note: Rare 1-cost attack: damage + Forge. Premium pick. Strong because it's a Forge card that also deals damage — efficient turn 1-2 play. Upgrades increase both damage and Forge value. Pair with Sword Sage / Seeking Edge to amplify each Forge stack.
Common 1-cost attack: damage + Forge. The reliable common Forge attack. Multiple copies are fine — each one stacks Sovereign Blade. Good turn 1 play after Sovereign Blade has spawned.

Common 1-cost skill: Forge + next-turn energy bonus. Turn 1 play — banks energy for a Blade-spending turn 2. Excellent in any Forge deck; the energy carryover lets you queue Sovereign Blade + other plays next turn.

Uncommon 2-cost skill: Block + Forge. Defense-and-Forge in one card. The 2-cost is steep but the dual value is worth it in elite/boss fights. Avoid in tempo-heavy hallway fights where 2 energy is too much budget.

Rare 1-cost skill: Forge (high). The dedicated Forge skill — biggest single Forge value per energy. Premium pick. Take whenever offered; multiple copies snowball into 30+ damage Sovereign Blades by mid-Act 2.
Common 1-cost skill: Forge + draw. Cycle and Forge in one. Strong tempo card; the draw helps surface Sovereign Blade or another payoff.
Rare 0-cost skill: draw + energy + Forge. 0-cost Forge with bonus energy and cycling — the best Forge tempo card. Take always when offered. Plays for free, stacks Blade, fuels next attack.
1-cost skill: Forges and doubles Sovereign Blade's damage this turn. Premium finisher for Forge decks, enabling massive burst lethal against high-HP targets before they can strike.

note_history.note: 1-cost skill: Forges and doubles Sovereign Blade's damage this turn. Premium finisher for Forge decks, enabling massive burst lethal against high-HP targets before they can strike.

note: Uncommon 1-cost skill: Forge + put Sovereign Blade in hand. Solves the 'Blade is in draw pile' problem — takes the deck-cycle randomness out of when the buffed Blade swings. Highly valued in any Forge deck, especially with Sword Sage / Seeking Edge active.

Rare 2-cost power: when you Forge, all allies Forge as well. Multiplier in multiplayer / Wraith setups. In single-player it just stacks Blade twice on each Forge play (if you have an ally summon). Skip in solo decks unless using Bound Phylactery. Re-evaluate if multiplayer.

Uncommon 1-cost power: Forge at start of each turn. Passive Forge engine — prints free Sovereign Blade damage every turn it's active. Premium take. Best when paired with Sword Sage so each turn's Forge applies a permanent buff to a multi-hit Blade.
Rare 1-cost power: Forges and makes Sovereign Blade deal damage to ALL enemies. Absolutely essential for Forge decks to solve multi-enemy fights.

note: Rare 2-cost power: Sovereign Blade hits an additional time per copy. Multiplicative with Forge stacks. Two copies → Blade hits 3 times. Critical for any Forge deck — the difference between a 30-damage Blade and a 90-damage Blade. Take always.

Uncommon 1-cost power: gain Block whenever you play Sovereign Blade. Defense-via-Blade. Strong in long fights where the Blade is played multiple times. Less valuable when the Blade is exhausted or when fights end in 2-3 turns.

Rare 2-cost power: gain Stars at the start of each turn. Premium star engine. Take every time. Pays off the 2-energy cost over 2-3 turns; afterward it's pure value. Required for any star-spender deck and useful even in Forge-only decks for the flexibility.

Uncommon 1-cost power: passively generates stars under some condition. Solid mid-tier provider; less raw value than Genesis but cheaper. Take in star-spender decks; skip in pure Forge decks if star-cost cards aren't drafted.
Uncommon 0-cost skill: gain Stars (high). Free star income. Strong as a draft pick but exhaust-on-play means each copy is one-shot. Pair with star-cost rares to get value out of the star burst.
Common 1-cost skill: gain Stars now + bonus next turn. Cheapest reliable provider. Stack 2 copies for a steady star economy. Less star-per-energy than Royal Gamble but renewable.
Common 1-cost skill: gain Stars + draw + scaling buff next turn. Multi-purpose: provides stars, cycles, sets up next turn. Decent take — better in star-spender decks that need turn-2 burst.
Common 1-cost attack: damage + gain Stars. The basic damage-and-provider attack. Solid early pick — turns hallway fights into star fuel for elites/bosses.
Uncommon 1-cost attack: damage + Stars + put on top of draw. Recurring damage + provider. Strong in any deck. The top-of-draw effect makes it reliable next turn.
Uncommon 1-cost skill: next turn gain Energy + Stars (Retain). Setup card — banks resources for turn 2-3. Strong in slow decks; weak when the deck wants to act-now.

Uncommon 3-cost attack: high damage + gain Stars on kill. Execute card. Spend stars on this in elite/boss fights to finish off lieutenants and refund stars from the kill. Skip if no star-cost payoffs in deck — 3 energy for damage alone is poor value.

Uncommon 0-energy + ★ star-cost attack. The reference star-spender. NEVER take without 2+ providers — without star fuel this is a brick. With Genesis or Royal Gamble in deck, becomes a turn 1 free 0-energy attack.
Uncommon 0-cost AOE attack: damage to ALL enemies for each Star gained this turn. Burst-damage payoff for star-spike turns. Pair with Royal Gamble + Convergence for explosive AOE rounds.

Uncommon 1-cost power: gain Block whenever you spend Stars. Defense scaling for star-spender decks. Take when the deck has 3+ star-cost cards; skip in pure Forge decks. Stacks well with Royal Gamble + Stardust for an attacker/defender swing turn.
Ancient 1-cost power: rare + powerful star generator. Endgame win condition for star-spender decks. Take if offered as ancient relic / ancient card reward — pivots the deck into pure star-spend mode.
Rare 1-cost power: scaling damage / value over the run. Not Regent-specific economy but a strong late-game power. Take in any deck.
Rare 1-cost skill: massive Block / temporary invuln effect. Boss-survival panic button. Take 1 copy in any Regent deck for boss-stage anti-burst.
Uncommon 0-cost skill: significant Block. The premier 0-cost defense card. Universal pick — slots into Forge, star-spender, and balanced Regent decks alike.
Rare 0-cost attack: damage scaling with deck conditions. Free damage card. Take in any deck. Synergizes with star-providers because 0-cost lets you spend stars + energy on a separate payoff.
Rare 0-cost attack: high damage + draw. Tempo + cycle. Universal premium pick — no constraints, no setup.

Rare 3-cost AOE attack: very high damage to all enemies. Boss-fight finisher. 3 energy is steep — only take if the deck has reliable energy generation (Refine Blade, Convergence) or in slow boss-grind decks.
Rare 2-cost attack: high damage + scaling effect on Forge plays. Synergizes with the Forge engine. Take in Forge decks; skip in pure star-spender.
Rare 1-cost attack: high damage + drawback. Single-target spike. Take in any deck for raw damage.
Rare 1-cost power: scaling buff (likely Strength-like). Take in long-fight decks. Not Regent-specific but adds passive damage to every attack including Sovereign Blade.
Rare 3-cost power: late-game scaling power. Long-fight win condition. Take in slow / Genesis-anchored decks; skip in tempo decks where 3 energy can't be spared.
Rare 0-cost skill: choose-an-effect / branching utility. Versatile — situationally strong. Take 1 copy.
Rare 1-cost skill: high-tempo utility (specifics depend on flavor text). Solid take in any deck.
Rare 2-cost skill: large defensive effect / summon. Strong in long fights; the 2-cost is fine in slow decks. Skip in tempo decks.
Rare 1-cost skill: bundle / multi-card payoff. Take if drafted; passive value.
Rare 0-cost attack: fast utility damage. Free action. Take always.
Ancient 0-cost attack: massive AOE / scaling. Endgame finisher. If offered as ancient reward, pivots deck into AOE blowout territory.

Token 2-cost colorless attack — base 10 damage. Cannot be drafted; only enters hand via Forge. Each Forge play after the first stacks +damage on ALL copies of Sovereign Blade (in hand, draw, discard, exhaust). Exhausting or transforming the Blade resets future Forges to a fresh 10-damage copy. CORE RULES: (1) play Forge cards BEFORE Sovereign Blade each turn, (2) NEVER exhaust/transform a buffed Blade, (3) blade-buff powers (Sword Sage, Seeking Edge) apply to newly-spawned Blades — re-forging after they're active is safe, even productive, if forced to lose the Blade.

note: 1 energy deal 10 initially. 'Balanced numberwise and Regent hates balanced cards.' With heavy star payoffs becomes ~1 energy 16 — but the deck shouldn't have that many payoffs in early game. Preferably not picked.

Situational AOE attack — efficient in AOE fights, bad otherwise. Mainly picked in Underdocks for Phantasmal Gardeners. Astral Pulse exists and is also common — if Astral Pulse is already in deck, Crush Under is still pickable for AOE supplement. If Crush Under is in deck first, still take Astral Pulse on top.
1 energy deal 10 — balanced, therefore bad on Regent. Very good upgrade but you usually have something better to upgrade in act 1. Some uses with Kingly cards but generally better not in deck.
2 energy deal 18 with Vigor/relic synergy. Balanced number, therefore bad. Better with existing synergy (Miniature Cannon, Akabeko, Prep Time) but not worth picking up speculatively.
0 energy block 7 + cheap star cost. 'Extremely solid pick early game.' Star cost low enough that it doesn't mess with overall economy.

0 energy debuff card. 'Debuffs are broken especially Weakness.' Multiple copies reduce the need for Gamma Blast/Comet (which require additional star gen). Endgame deck often has 2-3 copies, with upgrades for permanent boss debuffs. PRIMARY FARM TARGET.

1 energy deal 6 + scaling + self-cycle draw + card-creation synergy. Raw numbers weak now but transforms curses/statuses, very good vs Soul Fysh. Looping decks (small deck, same strong turn every turn) appreciate the trim. Situational — good when good.

1 energy block 6 + utility. Balanced number. Better off not in deck unless utility is essential (Sovereign Blade + Parry is your whole damage, or deck has no damage without a Kingly card). Was wrongly graded A in the prior tier list.

Pretty good block common but 2-energy cost is awkward when relying on Sovereign Blade for damage. Slightly better in Overgrowth for Slither + Byrd Swoop synergy. XecnaR prefers to greed-skip; slightly better than Patter.

Really good if you can play it — but competes with Astral Pulse and Reflect for limited star gen. XecnaR prefers Know Thy Place for debuffs (smoother star management). Still very pickable, especially if short on boss damage.
Really bad — only pops off with Vigor/Strength + a big-skill turn (Brightest Flame/Relax) AND low deck damage. OK as a Vantom counter if deck looks bad against it and the fight is soon.
Cards costing more than 3 stars create bad turn-1 draw orders and reliance on them is awkward. Devastate deals lots of damage but is replaceable. XecnaR prefers not to have it in deck.
Slow and weaker than Kingly Kick as a boss solution. Numbers are fair = bad. Pickable early game but pretty awkward to play into. XecnaR prefers to greed-skip.
2 self-refund energy 15 damage — good on paper but awkward to spend frontload 2 energy and easy to float energy next turn. Decent early game attack, good Slither target in Overgrowth.

Better of the Kingly duo. Scaling to 0 energy makes topend better (enemies don't have infinite HP); first draw is also better frontload-wise (kills small enemies in AOE). Awkward with Cosmic Indifference (can't play both same turn). XecnaR picks this as a boss solution often; falls off in act 3.
Simple — if you make too much stars and don't have enough energy, it's good. Not worth picking speculatively.
Basically useless unless deck mainly deals damage with Lunar Blast or Radiate (the 1-big-turn strategy). High priority upgrade if it's doing something.

Colorless choose-1-of-3 — colorless card pool is insanely strong in Spire 2 and choose-1-of-3 means high hit rate. 2 stars = low commitment; with starter relic + 1 Venerate play you can fire Quasar AND a 3-star payoff like Astral Pulse same cycle. Extremely strong upgrade. Can be your single source of scaling. Blocks with Child + Pillar of Creation. PRIMARY FARM TARGET.

Transforms any 2 cards in draw pile — crazy strong concept. Transforming 2 Strikes is at least 1 energy 14+ delayed damage while improving the reshuffle. Transforms bad statuses and curses. Card-creation synergy. 'Just a very good card overall at every point in the run.' PRIMARY FARM TARGET.

Spending 1 energy to draw is hard to justify early game even with star setup. Often pretty good lategame but XecnaR doesn't love carrying it early when the priority is hallway strength. Good synergy with Kingly cards.
Balanced numberwise but card-creation synergy is strong. Indifferent — never really too bad or too good.

Overstatted block-with-spike-damage star-cost skill. XecnaR's view: 'Stupid broken overstatted garbage card why is this in the game. If you've played Reflect before you know how dumb it is.' ONE OF THE PRIMARY CARDS TO FARM FOR in 0.103 — autopick when offered. (0.104.0 will nerf -1 block which moves it to unpickable, but we are on 0.103.)

Same costs as Reflect, pretty funny joke. Almost never worth spending stars on. Even best fights (Entomancer, act 3 bosses) it's underwhelming. Maybe consider if heavy star gen + no boss damage + pre-upgraded.
Draw-order dependent with mediocre payoff. Like Conqueror, tricks you with big numbers when things line up. Just pick Patter if you have strong multihit.

Drawing lots without playing them is bad early. Becomes strong with energy generation (Void Form, Orbit, Automation, Alignment+ loops). Really good Touch of Insanity target. Speculative without enabler.

Doesn't do anything early game. Effect can be strong upgraded lategame (proc → 2 extra draws → consistent re-procs). Other powers have better lategame payoffs while being better early. You can only carry so many powers.
THE OTHER PREMIUM BLOCK POWER. Like Child of the Stars: extremely high output, many ways to proc. PRIMARY FARM TARGET — absolutely worth carrying speculatively early game.
Very good power but not easy to play early without relic support and the 2-cost before upgrade. Don't love carrying super early over the broken powers.

PREMIUM POWER. Colorless card pool is insanely strong in Spire 2 — this card almost singlehandedly solves every long fight in early game. Easiest way to consistently proc Pillar of Creation (colorless cards make other cards too). ABSOLUTELY WORTH PICKING EARLY. Note: random colorless doesn't reliably solve boss damage on its own — pair with Supermassive/Arsenal for boss output. PRIMARY FARM TARGET.

Crush Under cranked to 11. Amazing in AOE fights or bosses that multihit (notably Test Subject phase 2). Ethereal makes it a bit awkward but still really strong. One of the rares XecnaR would accept from Neow's curse-for-rare bonus.

Reliable boss damage engine with Innate upgrade and bosses not hitting hard turn 1. Pure colorless-generation isn't a fully consistent boss-damage plan, so this card has its place — most commonly end of act 2 when deck output is just Spectrum Shift.
Hard to get in play and doesn't block nearly as much as the good block powers. Pretty bad.
1-cost status generated by Collision Course. Plays to exhaust, meaning you must pay a 1-energy tax to clear it from your hand. Keep this economy drain in mind before spamming Collision Course.
